answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
  Core job features such as skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback predict internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports taking interesting, autonomous work very seriously, but it does not imply that interest is the only relevant dimension.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
  This meta-analysis finds that motivating work characteristics—especially autonomy, task significance, feedback, and complexity—are strongly related to satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and performance. It also shows that social context and working conditions matter, so “interesting work” is important but not exhaustive.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999)**  
  Tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks when they feel controlling. This supports caution about optimizing narrowly for pay when the current work is intrinsically compelling.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
  Intrinsic motivation predicts performance especially well for complex, quality-focused work. Extrinsic incentives still add predictive value, so the evidence does not support focusing on intrinsic interest “almost exclusively.”

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
  Objective pay has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, while satisfaction with pay matters more than absolute pay. This suggests a slightly higher salary may be less important than differences in work content, autonomy, and meaning.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay (2019)**  
  Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, life satisfaction, and lower turnover intentions. This supports the view that meaningful or interesting work is often underweighted in career decisions.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
  People who experience their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. The paper supports purpose-aligned work, but it does not show that salary, role, or career growth should be ignored.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009)**  
  Deeply meaningful work can lead people to tolerate lower pay, worse conditions, and self-sacrifice. This is a warning against treating interesting or mission-driven work as the sole criterion.

- **“Work Engagement: A Quantitative Review and Test of Its Relations with Task and Contextual Performance” — Christian, Garza & Slaughter (2011)**  
  Work engagement is positively related to both task performance and broader organizational contribution. Interesting, meaningful, resource-rich work can therefore be instrumentally valuable, not merely pleasant.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Examination of the Relationship Between Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being” — Bowling, Eschleman & Wang (2010)**  
  Job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being are substantially related. Because work occupies so much of life, day-to-day interest and satisfaction can meaningfully affect overall well-being.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone (2006)**  
  People often overestimate how much higher income will improve happiness because focusing on income makes it seem more important than it is in daily experience. This is relevant when comparing a modest pay increase against a large change in work content.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
  Income generally continues to predict higher emotional well-being for many people, but the gains are smaller and more conditional than simple salary comparisons suggest. This argues that money matters, but usually not enough by itself to dominate work quality at already high income levels.

- **“What Do You Think Would Make You Happier? What Do You Think You Would Choose?” — Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012)**  
  People’s choices are strongly related to predicted happiness, but they also trade off happiness against status, control, purpose, family, and other values. This suggests the right decision may depend on more than “interestingness,” even if interestingness is central.

- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999)**  
  People tend to overestimate the motivational power of extrinsic incentives, especially when predicting others’ behavior. This supports the claim that intrinsic interest can be underrated, though the paper is not a direct career-choice study.

- **“Job Satisfaction and Promotions” — Kosteas (2011)**  
  Promotions and expected promotions are positively associated with job satisfaction, partly through recognition, status, and future opportunity. This suggests that leading a team may have real value beyond pay, especially if management is something you intrinsically want.

- **“Les Passions de l’âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al. (2003)**  
  Harmonious passion is associated with well-being and flexible persistence, while obsessive passion is associated with conflict and poorer outcomes. This cautions that pursuing interesting work is best when it remains balanced rather than all-consuming.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Anthropic - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
**Gathering research papers**

The user is asking if research shows that doing interesting things is underrated and if one should focus almost exclusively on that. I need to compile relevant paper names about career satisfaction, including topics like intrinsic motivation, person-job fit, and meaningful work. Each paper will get a two-sentence summary. I should avoid providing any advice and address the question indirectly through the summaries, emphasizing that while intrinsic factors matter, there are other important elements too.
**Compiling research papers**

I’m looking for relevant research papers that explore the relationship between interesting work and job satisfaction. One possible paper is “What Makes for a Dream Job? A Quantitative Analysis of the Roles of Interesting Work and Income in Job Satisfaction.” Additionally, I found a few other significant studies: Judge et al. (2010) highlight that pay has a modest correlation with job satisfaction, while Humphrey et al. (2007) discuss several factors like autonomy and social support as strong predictors of satisfaction. Fried & Ferris (1987) also review job characteristics impacting motivation and satisfaction.
**Exploring intrinsic motivation**

I have another relevant paper to consider: Deci, Koestner, and Ryan (1999) conducted a meta-analytic review on how extrinsic rewards impact intrinsic motivation. They found that tangible rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation for tasks that are already interesting, particularly when those rewards are expected or contingent. This insight adds an important layer to understanding how external factors might affect job satisfaction and engagement. It's all about balancing intrinsic and extrinsic motivations!
**Evaluating income and well-being**

I'm checking my memory for research findings on pay and job satisfaction. A meta-analysis of 86 studies shows a weak correlation between pay level and both job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. It's intriguing that high pay isn't a strong predictor of job satisfaction. Kahneman and Deaton (2010) found that while life evaluation improves with income up to about $75k, emotional well-being plateaus. However, Killingsworth (2021) argues that experienced well-being continues to rise even beyond that income level. Interesting nuances here!
**Analyzing work design and motivation**

I'm reviewing insights from research on work design. Humphrey et al. (2007) conducted a meta-analysis of 259 studies, noting that motivational characteristics—like autonomy, feedback, task significance, skill variety, and task identity—are strongly linked to job satisfaction and performance. For managing small teams, social and contextual aspects might boost performance independently of research interest. Fried and Ferris (1987) confirmed core job characteristics positively influence satisfaction and motivation. Lastly, Cerasoli et al. (2014) found that both intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives predict performance, emphasizing the value of intrinsic factors too.
**Evaluating motivation in research**

I'm considering the argument against focusing solely on interestingness in research. For highly creative tasks, intrinsic motivation really matters. Deci, Koestner, and Ryan's (1999) meta-analysis discusses how expected tangible rewards can actually undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. However, it's interesting that verbal rewards might enhance motivation instead. It seems that when it comes to keeping creativity alive, understanding intrinsic motivation and its influences is crucial!
**Evaluating career aspiration insights**

I’m thinking about exploring the topic of career aspirations. The contrast between technical specialists and managers is intriguing. Maybe I could use "Becoming a Manager: How New Managers Master the Challenges." The Peter Principle doesn’t seem essential here. I should definitely consider the findings from "The Value of Bosses" by Lazear, Shaw, and Stanton from 2015, which emphasizes that better bosses can significantly boost productivity, highlighting the importance of effective leadership.
answer:
**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang, and Morgeson find that autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, and social support strongly predict job satisfaction and motivation. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously, but defines it broadly enough to include leadership, autonomy, impact, and team quality.

**The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis** — Fried and Ferris find that jobs with autonomy, variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback are reliably linked to satisfaction and internal motivation. The implication is that “interesting” work matters a lot, but not as a single dimension divorced from responsibility, feedback, and perceived impact.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin, and Ford find that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially performance quality, while extrinsic incentives also matter, especially for quantity. This argues for prioritizing intrinsic interest in creative research work, but not for ignoring compensation or career structure entirely.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner, and Ryan find that expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, while informational praise can enhance it. This supports caution about choosing mainly for salary or status when the current work is deeply engaging.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, and Rich find that pay level is only weakly related to overall job satisfaction. This suggests a modest salary increase is often less important than day-to-day work quality, autonomy, and fit.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** — Kahneman and Deaton find that income is more strongly related to life evaluation than to daily emotional experience, with diminishing returns for emotional well-being. This weakens the case for treating a marginal salary increase as decisive once material needs are already well met.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises With Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** — Killingsworth finds that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, contrary to a strict plateau view. However, the relationship is roughly logarithmic, so a small percentage salary increase may matter less than a large change in work content.

**Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion** — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, and Stone argue that people overestimate the effect of income on happiness when they focus attention on money. This is directly relevant to offers where salary is salient but the daily texture of the work may dominate lived experience.

**Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover** — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka, and Lanivich find that vocational interests predict performance and turnover beyond many standard selection variables. This supports the idea that genuine interest is not just a luxury; it affects persistence and effectiveness.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, and Johnson find that fit predicts job satisfaction, commitment, intent to quit, and performance. This suggests the key question is not simply “interesting versus higher pay,” but which role fits your values, preferred activities, colleagues, and trajectory.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, and Schwartz show that people differ in whether they experience work mainly as a job, career, or calling. If your current research feels like a calling, sacrificing interest may be unusually costly; if leading people feels like a calling, the management opportunity may be intrinsically valuable.

**Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity** — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby, and Herron find that creativity is supported by challenge, autonomy, resources, encouragement, and supportive supervision, and hindered by organizational impediments and excessive pressure. This suggests “interesting research” matters most when paired with an environment that lets you actually do creative work.

**The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions** — Grant finds that seeing the human impact of one’s work can increase motivation, persistence, and performance. This implies that meaningfulness and perceived impact may matter as much as intellectual novelty when evaluating two research-lab roles.

**The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review** — Judge, Thoresen, Bono, and Patton find a positive relationship between job satisfaction and job performance. This makes work enjoyment and interest practically relevant, not merely hedonic, but it does not imply they should be optimized to the exclusion of role scope, leadership growth, or institutional fit.

answer:
- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature”** — This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and job complexity are strongly associated with job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It supports prioritizing interesting, high-autonomy work, but not “almost exclusively,” because social support and work context also matter.

- **“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis”** — This paper finds that fit between a person’s vocational interests and their job is reliably associated with higher job satisfaction. The effect is meaningful but not overwhelming, so interest fit is important without being the sole determinant of a good career move.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research”** — This meta-analysis finds that people tend to perform better when their work aligns with their interests. The relationship is positive but moderate, implying that interesting work helps but does not dominate factors like ability, role quality, team, and incentives.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis”** — This meta-analysis finds that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality and creativity-oriented performance. It also finds that extrinsic incentives still matter, especially for quantity and goal-directed output, so the evidence favors balance rather than pure intrinsic-interest maximization.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation”** — This paper finds that expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. It supports caution about chasing pay or title at the expense of fascination, though the effects depend on reward type and context.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being”** — This paper argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs underlying motivation and well-being. Interesting work matters most when it also supports agency, mastery, and connection, which could include either a research-heavy role or a team-lead role.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature”** — This meta-analysis finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. It suggests that a somewhat higher salary should not automatically outweigh substantially more interesting work, especially once compensation is already high.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved”** — This paper finds that emotional well-being generally continues to rise with income, but with diminishing and heterogeneous effects. It weakens the claim that salary is irrelevant, while still leaving room for day-to-day work quality to dominate at high income levels.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion”** — This paper argues that people often overestimate the happiness impact of income because money is salient when making comparisons. It is relevant because salary and title differences may loom larger during offer evaluation than they will in daily lived experience.

- **“Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation and Separate Evaluation”** — This paper shows that people overemphasize easy-to-compare differences when evaluating options side by side. In a job-choice context, salary and formal leadership title may be overweighted relative to how the work will actually feel day to day.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit”** — This meta-analysis finds that fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and turnover. It argues against optimizing only for task interestingness, because culture, manager, collaborators, and role fit are also empirically important.

answer:
- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath, 1999.** People tend to overestimate how much others are motivated by pay, bonuses, and status, and underestimate intrinsic motives like interest and meaning. This supports the claim that interesting work is often underrated, but not that it should be the only criterion.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.** Jobs are more motivating when they offer skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” maps strongly onto these factors, but leadership, autonomy, feedback, and impact matter too.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007.** Across many studies, motivational job features such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, and feedback predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. - **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci, 2000.** Well-being and motivation depend heavily on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. - **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.** Intrinsic motivation is a robust predictor of performance, especially for complex and quality-focused work. - **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.** Tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This cautions against trading away highly engaging work merely for somewhat higher compensation.

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.** Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the association is modest. This implies that a slightly higher salary is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in interest, fit, or autonomy.

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby & Herron, 1996.** Creativity is supported by challenging work, freedom, resources, supervisory encouragement, work-group support, and organizational encouragement. For research engineering, “interesting problems” matter a lot, but the surrounding team and management environment can amplify or suppress that value.

- **“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant, 2008.** Seeing how one’s work benefits others can increase motivation and performance. This suggests that perceived impact or mission may rival raw intellectual interestingness in importance.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.** Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover. The relevant question is therefore not just “Which work is more interesting?” but also “Where do I fit better overall?”

- **“Job Satisfaction and Promotions” — Kosteas, 2011.** Promotions and expected promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction, beyond pay effects alone. This supports treating the chance to lead a small team as a real positive, not merely a status perk.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009.** Deeply meaningful work can produce commitment, identity, and fulfillment, but also self-exploitation and tolerance of worse conditions. This is the main caution against making “interesting work” or “calling” an almost exclusive decision rule.

answer:
- **Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially when work is complex and quality-focused. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence supports weighting interesting work heavily, not ignoring salary or role structure.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation — Deci, Koestner, & Ryan (1999).** For tasks people already find interesting, controlling rewards can reduce later intrinsic motivation. Compensation and status are safest when they support autonomy rather than making the work feel externally controlled.

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting” work captures only part of this bundle; autonomy, meaning, and feedback also matter.

- **The Work Design Questionnaire: Developing and Validating a Comprehensive Measure for Assessing Job Design and the Nature of Work — Morgeson & Humphrey (2006).** Work quality depends on task, knowledge, social, and contextual characteristics. - **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005).** Fit strongly predicts job satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. This supports choosing work aligned with interests and values, but also checking fit with the manager, team, and institution.

- **Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012).** People perform better and persist longer when their work matches their vocational interests. This is one of the cleaner findings supporting the idea that “interesting” work is not a luxury variable.

- **Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model — Sheldon & Elliot (1999).** Goals aligned with a person’s interests and values lead to more sustained effort, higher attainment, and greater well-being. A role that advances self-concordant goals is likely to feel better over time than one chosen mainly for external rewards.

- **Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin, & Schwartz (1997).** People who experience their work as a calling report higher job and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career. The same occupation can be experienced differently, so meaning depends on both the role and personal values.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010).** Pay level is only weakly to moderately related to job satisfaction and pay satisfaction. This suggests a higher salary often has less impact than day-to-day work quality, especially once financial needs are comfortably met.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023).** Experienced well-being generally rises with income, including above previously proposed plateaus, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and heterogeneous. Salary is real but usually marginal at high income levels relative to large differences in work content or fit.

- **Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, & Stone (2006).** People tend to overestimate how much income differences will affect happiness because focusing on money makes it seem disproportionately important. This is relevant to career choices where salary and title are salient but daily activities dominate lived experience.

- **The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions — Grant (2008).** Seeing how one’s work benefits others can increase persistence and performance. A leadership role may compensate for less technical interestingness if it meaningfully increases impact, mentorship, or responsibility.

- **Job Demands–Resources Theory: Taking Stock and Looking Forward — Bakker & Demerouti (2017).** Engagement rises when jobs provide resources such as autonomy, support, feedback, and development opportunities, while burnout rises with excessive demands. This cautions against focusing almost exclusively on interestingness if the role changes workload, politics, or emotional strain.

answer:
**“Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976)**  
Jobs with skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback tend to produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports weighting “interesting work” heavily, but treats it as one part of broader work design rather than the only factor.

**“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007)**  
A large meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and job complexity all relate to satisfaction and performance. Interestingness matters, but leadership, team context, autonomy, and impact can matter independently.

**“The ‘What’ and ‘Why’ of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior” — Deci & Ryan (2000)**  
Well-being and sustained motivation are strongly linked to autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Interesting research may satisfy autonomy and competence, while leading a team may satisfy autonomy and relatedness, so the literature does not imply optimizing only for intellectual interest.

**“Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation” — Gagné & Deci (2005)**  
Work motivation is highest when people experience their work as autonomous, meaningful, and aligned with internal values. Extrinsic rewards and titles are not worthless, but they are less reliable motivators when they crowd out autonomy or intrinsic engagement.

**“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014)**  
Intrinsic motivation robustly predicts performance, especially for complex and quality-oriented work. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the evidence favors giving intrinsic interest major weight, not exclusive weight.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010)**  
Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. This suggests that a somewhat higher salary should usually not dominate a decision when the work itself differs substantially in interest or fit.

**“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023)**  
Higher income is associated with higher experienced well-being for many people, though the marginal gains are relatively small and vary by baseline happiness. This weakens any claim that salary should be ignored, but also weakens the idea that salary should dominate.

**“Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover” — Van Iddekinge, Roth, Putka & Lanivich (2011)**  
Vocational interests predict performance, training performance, and turnover-related outcomes. This supports taking deep interest seriously as a career variable, especially in cognitively demanding roles.

**“Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis” — Hoff, Song, Wee, Phan & Rounds (2020)**  
Fit between a person’s interests and their work is positively related to job satisfaction. The effect is meaningful but not total, implying interest fit is important alongside other forms of fit.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005)**  
Person–job, person–organization, person–group, and person–supervisor fit all predict satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. A role can be intellectually interesting but still poor overall if team, organization, or manager fit is weak.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997)**  
People who experience their work as a calling report higher work and life satisfaction than those who see it mainly as a job or career ladder. The relevant variable is meaningful identity and purpose, not merely novelty or technical fascination.

**“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski (2010)**  
Meaningful work comes from multiple sources, including the self, others, the work context, and broader purpose. This implies that leading a team could be meaningful even if the object-level work is less interesting.

**“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001)**  
Employees often reshape their tasks, relationships, and interpretations to make work more meaningful. A less-interesting role may become more interesting if it has high agency, but a rigid role may not.

**“Decision and Experience: Why Don’t We Choose What Makes Us Happy?” — Hsee & Hastie (2006)**  
People often overweight easily compared attributes and underweight what their future experience will actually feel like. This supports caution about salary, title, and prestige, but does not prove that interestingness should be optimized almost exclusively.

**“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018)**  
Believing interests are simply “found” can reduce openness and persistence when work becomes difficult. The paper argues for evaluating whether a role can develop into deeper interest, not only whether it is maximally interesting upfront.

  Controlling tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, while informational feedback and praise can increase it. This cautions against taking a higher salary or leadership title if it comes with reduced autonomy or more controlling work conditions.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, social support, and work context all predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. “Interesting work” matters a lot, but the broader design of the job matters too.

- **Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  Jobs are more motivating when they provide skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. This implies that a less “interesting” role could still be highly motivating if it offers ownership, impact, and growth, while an interesting role can disappoint if those are absent.

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
  Fit between a person and their job, organization, team, and supervisor strongly predicts job satisfaction and commitment. For this decision, fit with the research agenda, leadership responsibilities, company culture, and manager/team may all be relevant.

- **Interest Fit and Job Satisfaction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis**  
  People whose vocational interests match their work tend to be more satisfied, though the effects are meaningful rather than all-dominating. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously, but not treating it as the sole criterion.

- **Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research**  
  Vocational interests predict job performance, training performance, and persistence across many studies. Interest is therefore not just about enjoyment; it can affect long-term effectiveness and career success.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  Pay level has a relatively modest relationship with job satisfaction, while satisfaction with pay matters more but still does not explain everything. A somewhat higher salary is valuable, but it may not compensate for a large drop in intrinsic motivation or role fit.

- **Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved**  
  Well-being generally continues to rise with income, but the relationship varies across people and is not the whole story. This argues against ignoring salary entirely, while also suggesting that at already-high compensation levels, job content may dominate marginal pay differences.

- **The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout**  
  Burnout is driven by high demands and insufficient resources, while engagement is supported by autonomy, feedback, support, and meaningful goals. A leadership role can be energizing if it adds agency and impact, or draining if it mainly adds coordination load and stress.

- **Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead**  
  People differ in how much they genuinely want to lead, and motivation to lead predicts leadership experience and development. If leading a small team is a deep personal goal, that is a real person-role fit factor rather than merely an extrinsic perk.

answer:
- **On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives**  
  People systematically overestimate how much others are motivated by money and other external rewards, and underestimate intrinsic motivation. This supports your friends’ “interesting work is underrated” claim, but not the stronger “focus on it almost exclusively” claim.

- **Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory**  
  Hackman and Oldham show that autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback predict internal motivation and job satisfaction. This implies that intellectually rich, autonomous work is a major driver of long-run work satisfaction.

- **Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature**  
  This meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, social support, and manageable work context all relate to satisfaction, motivation, and performance. It supports prioritizing interesting work, but also says social environment and role design matter a lot.

- **Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being**  
  Ryan and Deci argue that well-being and high-quality motivation come from autonomy, competence, and relatedness. “Interesting work” matters especially when it satisfies these needs, but leadership, team quality, and culture can satisfy them too.

- **Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do**  
  Amabile argues that intrinsic motivation is especially important for creativity and complex problem-solving. For research-heavy roles, this is strong evidence that genuinely interesting work has unusually high value.

- **Are You Interested? A Meta-Analysis of Relations Between Vocational Interests and Employee Performance and Turnover**  
  Vocational interests predict job performance, training performance, and lower turnover. This suggests that alignment with what you actually find interesting is not just about happiness; it also affects effectiveness and persistence.

- **A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation**  
  Deci, Koestner, and Ryan find that controlling extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for already-interesting tasks. This cautions against over-weighting compensation, though ordinary salary increases are not the same as controlling rewards.

- **The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature**  
  Judge and colleagues find that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. This supports the idea that a somewhat higher salary often matters less than day-to-day work quality once pay is already good.

- **The Importance of Pay in Employee Motivation: Discrepancies Between What People Say and What They Do**  
  Rynes, Gerhart, and Minette argue that people often understate the importance of pay, while behavior shows pay can affect attraction, retention, and motivation. This is a counterweight to “ignore salary almost entirely.”

- **Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit**  
  Kristof-Brown and colleagues find that fit with the job, organization, group, and supervisor strongly predicts satisfaction, commitment, and turnover intentions. The relevant question is broader than “interesting versus less interesting”: it includes fit with mission, people, management style, and identity.

- **The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions**  
  Grant shows that seeing the impact of one’s work on beneficiaries can improve motivation and performance. If one role feels less intellectually interesting but more impactful or people-oriented, that can partly compensate.

- **The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art**  
  Bakker and Demerouti argue that engagement and burnout depend on the balance between job resources and job demands. A team-lead role may add autonomy, influence, and growth, but also coordination load, interruptions, and emotional demands.

- **Toward a Theory of Individual Differences and Leadership: Understanding the Motivation to Lead**  
  Chan and Drasgow show that people differ meaningfully in motivation to lead, and that this predicts leadership-related outcomes. If leading a team is a deep intrinsic goal for you, the management component may itself be “interesting work.”

- **Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion**  
  Kahneman and colleagues argue that people often overestimate the effect of income on experienced well-being because money becomes salient during comparison. This suggests being careful not to overweight a modest salary increase when comparing daily work lives.

- **Job Satisfaction and Subjective Well-Being: A Meta-Analysis**  
  Bowling and colleagues find a meaningful relationship between job satisfaction and overall subjective well-being. Since work occupies a large share of life, choosing work you find engaging and satisfying can have broad life consequences.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Jobs with autonomy, skill variety, task significance, task identity, and feedback tend to produce higher internal motivation and satisfaction. This supports prioritizing interesting, meaningful work, but as part of a broader work-design bundle rather than as the only factor.

- **“The Validity of the Job Characteristics Model: A Review and Meta-Analysis” — Fried & Ferris (1987).** This meta-analysis found that enriched, motivating job characteristics are reliably related to satisfaction and motivation. The evidence favors taking “interesting work” seriously, though effects on performance are more mixed than effects on satisfaction.

- **“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007).** Autonomy, task significance, feedback, social support, and work context all predict satisfaction, motivation, and performance. Interesting work matters a lot, but team environment, support, and role design also matter substantially.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).** People perform better and persist more when their work matches their interests. The effect is meaningful but not so dominant that interest should crowd out all other considerations.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005).** Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower intent to quit. This suggests “interesting work” is one important kind of fit, but manager/team/org fit can be equally decision-relevant.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially for complex and creative tasks. Extrinsic incentives also predict performance, so the literature does not support ignoring salary, title, or advancement entirely.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999).** Expected, tangible, performance-contingent rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This supports caution about chasing compensation at the expense of curiosity, but it does not imply compensation is irrelevant.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).** Pay level is only weakly to moderately related to job satisfaction, while satisfaction depends heavily on non-pay factors. This supports discounting a modest salary increase when the alternative has much more compelling work.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023).** Higher income is associated with higher well-being for many people, but the marginal value depends on baseline happiness and is not unlimited. The paper argues against “money doesn’t matter,” but also against treating money as the main driver once other conditions are strong.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997).** People differ in whether they experience work mainly as a job, career, or calling, and those orientations relate to satisfaction and meaning. If your current work feels closer to a calling, the psychological cost of leaving may be large.

- **“The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review” — Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton (2001).** Job satisfaction and job performance are positively related, though causality is complex. Interesting, satisfying work may support better long-term performance, especially in roles requiring creativity and sustained effort.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw & Stanton (2015).** Boss quality has large effects on worker productivity, showing that management roles can create real leverage. This makes leading a team a serious intrinsic and impact-related consideration, not merely a status perk.

**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012).**  
Vocational interests predict job performance, especially when interests match the tasks and environment. This supports taking “interesting work” seriously because interest can affect persistence and effectiveness.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010).**  
Pay level is only weakly to moderately related to job satisfaction. This suggests marginal salary increases may be less important than job content, fit, autonomy, and meaning once needs are met.

**“High Income Improves Evaluation of Life But Not Emotional Well-Being” — Kahneman & Deaton (2010).**  
Higher income improves life evaluation, but daily emotional well-being showed diminishing returns above a comfortable threshold in their data. The paper is often cited to argue that non-monetary job qualities can dominate once compensation is sufficient.

**“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth (2021).**  
This paper challenges the hard income plateau and finds experienced well-being continues rising with income on average. It weakens any claim that salary should be mostly ignored, though the income effect remains only one part of well-being.

**“On the Meaning of Work: A Theoretical Integration and Review” — Rosso, Dekas & Wrzesniewski (2010).**  
Meaningful work comes from sources including self-expression, impact on others, belonging, and alignment with values. A role that is less intellectually interesting but offers leadership, mentorship, or impact could still score highly on meaning.

**“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001).**  
People can reshape tasks, relationships, and interpretations of their jobs to make them more meaningful. This implies the “interestingness” of a role is not fixed and may depend on autonomy and ability to craft the work.

**“The Job Demands–Resources Model: State of the Art” — Bakker & Demerouti (2007).**  
Engagement rises when jobs provide resources like autonomy, feedback, support, and growth, while burnout rises when demands are high and resources are insufficient. Interesting work can energize, but excessive demands or poor support can still make a role bad.

**“The Value of Working Conditions in the United States and Implications for the Structure of Wages” — Maestas, Mullen, Powell, von Wachter & Wenger (2023).**  
Workers place substantial monetary value on non-wage job attributes such as autonomy, schedule flexibility, meaningfulness, and job security. The paper supports treating job quality as multi-dimensional rather than reducing the decision to either salary or interestingness.

answer:
**Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** — Hackman & Oldham, 1976  
Shows that jobs high in skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback create experienced meaningfulness and responsibility. These “interesting work” features predict motivation and satisfaction more directly than compensation does.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007  
A large meta-analysis finds that autonomy, task significance, task variety, feedback, and social support are strongly related to job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and performance. This supports the idea that day-to-day work content is a major driver of career well-being.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Judge et al., 2010  
Finds that pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once basic needs, fairness, and alternatives are accounted for.

**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014  
Intrinsic motivation is especially predictive of quality, creativity, and persistence, while extrinsic incentives are more predictive of quantity. The paper argues against focusing exclusively on either interest or pay: both matter, but for research-like work intrinsic motivation is particularly important.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999  
Expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation, especially for tasks people already find interesting. This is relevant when a role’s appeal is mostly salary or status rather than the work itself.

**Self-Determination Theory and Work Motivation** — Gagné & Deci, 2005  
Argues that autonomy, competence, and relatedness are core psychological needs that drive high-quality motivation at work. A leadership role could be valuable if it increases autonomy and competence, even if the technical work is less intrinsically interesting.

**Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity** — Amabile et al., 1996  
Finds that creativity is supported by challenging work, autonomy, resources, encouragement, and low organizational impediments. For research careers, the environment that lets you pursue interesting, challenging problems can directly affect creative output.

**Affect and Creativity at Work** — Amabile et al., 2005  
Shows that positive affect at work predicts creativity, including effects that carry over across days. If “interesting work” reliably produces better inner work life, it may compound into better research performance.

**The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions** — Grant, 2008  
Finds that seeing the human impact of one’s work can increase motivation and performance. This suggests that “interesting” may not be only intellectual novelty; perceived importance and mission can also matter.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — Wrzesniewski et al., 1997  
People who view their work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those who view it mainly as a job or career. The result supports prioritizing meaning, but not necessarily ignoring pay, status, or working conditions.

**The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work** — Bunderson & Thompson, 2009  
Meaningful work can produce deep satisfaction and identity, but it can also make people tolerate sacrifice, overwork, or undercompensation. This is a caution against treating interesting or meaningful work as the only variable.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005  
Person-job and person-organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, performance, and retention. The best choice may depend less on abstract salary-versus-interest and more on fit with the work, team, values, and desired role.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** — Kahneman & Deaton, 2010  
Finds that income continues to improve life evaluation, but emotional well-being showed diminishing returns past a high-enough income threshold in their data. For already-high earners, extra salary may have smaller day-to-day happiness effects than work quality.

**Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year** — Killingsworth, 2021  
Challenges the simple income-plateau story, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income for many people. Still, the relationship is roughly logarithmic, so each additional dollar tends to matter less at higher incomes.

**What Really Matters in a Job? Hedonic Measurement Using Quit Data** — Clark, 2001  
Uses quit behavior to infer which job attributes workers actually value, finding that non-pay aspects of work matter substantially. This supports taking job content, hours, security, autonomy, and satisfaction seriously rather than treating salary as the main signal.

- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham, 1976.**  
  The job-characteristics model argues that meaningfulness, responsibility, and feedback drive internal motivation and satisfaction. “Interesting work” is central, especially when it involves autonomy and skill variety, but its effect depends on individual growth needs.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014.**  
  Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives also predict performance, especially quantity. This supports valuing interesting work highly, but not ignoring salary or role incentives.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012.**  
  People perform better and persist more when their work matches their interests. The effects are meaningful but moderate, so interest fit matters a lot but is not the only variable.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005.**  
  Fit with the job, organization, team, and supervisor predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. Interesting work maps mostly to person–job fit, but organization and team fit can be similarly important.

- **“Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being” — Ryan & Deci, 2000.**  
  Well-being and high-quality motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A leadership role may satisfy autonomy and competence even if the technical content is less intrinsically interesting.

- **“Outcomes of Meaningful Work: A Meta-Analysis” — Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019.**  
  Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. If “interesting” also means meaningful and important, the evidence supports giving it substantial weight.

- **“The Relationship between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010.**  
  Pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. Higher salary alone is unlikely to compensate for substantially worse work content, though pay still matters for fairness, security, and goals.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023.**  
  Experienced well-being generally rises with income on a log scale, but the effect is heterogeneous and often smaller than people expect. This argues against treating salary as irrelevant, but also against over-weighting a modest raise.

- **“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999.**  
  Controlling extrinsic rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. The implication is not that money is bad, but that choices framed mainly around external rewards may reduce long-term engagement.

- **“The Job Demands–Resources Model of Burnout” — Demerouti, Bakker, Nachreiner & Schaufeli, 2001.**  
  Burnout is driven by high demands and low resources, while engagement is supported by autonomy, feedback, support, and growth opportunities. Interesting work can still become bad if demands are extreme, and less-interesting work can be sustainable if resources are excellent.

- **“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997.**  
  People who experience their work as a calling tend to report greater satisfaction and meaning than those who see it mainly as a job or career ladder. This supports prioritizing work that feels intrinsically compelling, but it does not show that advancement or leadership should be ignored.

- **“Affect and Creativity at Work” — Amabile, Barsade, Mueller & Staw, 2005.**

- **“Psychological Empowerment in the Workplace: Dimensions, Measurement, and Validation” — Spreitzer, 1995.**

I need to focus on gathering relevant paper names and crafting two-sentence summaries for each. Listing 12-15 papers with titles and authors might be effective, ensuring each summary is concise and precise. I'll keep the summaries to exactly two sentences only.
**Verifying citations and titles**

I need to check the citations and titles for accuracy. 

For instance, there's the paper titled "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature" by Judge et al. from 2010 in the Journal of Vocational Behavior. The findings suggest that the correlation between pay level and satisfaction is around 0.15, while the correlation between pay satisfaction and job satisfaction is higher at 0.22. The correlation at a national level averages around 0.17, which seems like a solid point. Let's keep this organized!
**Exploring job satisfaction factors**

I'm thinking about how person-job fit is linked to job satisfaction and performance. It involves interests, abilities, and values, but it's broader than just being interesting. I want to check a specific study titled "Vocational interests and performance: A quantitative summary of over 60 years of research". This paper explores how interests actually predict job performance and turnover. Additionally, there’s another study titled "The Search for Meaning in Work" that I want to consider.
**Searching for relevant papers**

I’m looking for a paper on the psychological effects of becoming a manager. There's one titled "Are managers happier? A study of work and non-work wellbeing" that might be pertinent. Also, I came across "Bosses are happier than workers: Evidence from 15 countries," but it seems less relevant. I could focus more on work design and self-determination theory instead. Let’s see where this leads!
**Exploring meaning in work**

I want to delve into how meaning comes from various aspects like tasks, missions, relationships, and identity, rather than just what’s interesting. When we consider jobs, careers, and callings, having a calling orientation often leads to higher satisfaction. It's intriguing that callings can be constructed or framed, not just something we inherently have. Also, I should look into how extrinsic rewards can impact intrinsic motivation, especially in interesting tasks, and how compensation affects autonomy.
**Refining paper summaries**

I should focus on providing relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries without framing them as questions. It seems important to avoid conclusions like “almost exclusively.” I’ll aim to include only pertinent papers and ensure each summary is concise, just two sentences. That sounds like a solid plan!
answer:
**Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality, creativity, and complex work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, so the evidence supports prioritizing interesting work heavily but not exclusively.

**The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature** — Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is relatively modest. This suggests that once compensation is already strong, differences in work content, autonomy, and fit may dominate a small salary increase.

**Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature** — Autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, and social support are consistently associated with job satisfaction and performance. “Interesting work” matters, but it is part of a broader bundle of job-design factors.

**Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit** — Fit with the job, organization, team, and manager predicts satisfaction, commitment, performance, and lower turnover intentions. The evidence supports asking not only “is the work interesting?” but also “does this environment fit me?”

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** — People tend to perform better and persist longer when their work aligns with their interests. This is strong evidence that doing genuinely interesting work is often underrated in career decisions.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** — Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central psychological needs underlying motivation and well-being. Interesting research may satisfy autonomy and competence, while leading a team may satisfy competence and relatedness if the role is well designed.

**A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation** — Expected tangible rewards can sometimes reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, especially when they feel controlling. This supports caution about overweighting salary if it comes with less autonomy or less meaningful work.

**The Search for Meaning in Work** — Meaningful work comes from several sources: the task itself, contribution to others, identity, values, relationships, and personal growth. Interestingness is important, but meaning can also come from mentoring, leadership, mission, and responsibility.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work** — People who view their work as a calling report greater satisfaction and connection to their work. The paper supports valuing deep interest and purpose, though it does not imply that job content should be the only consideration.

**Relational Job Design and the Motivation to Make a Prosocial Difference** — Work becomes more motivating when people can see how their efforts benefit others. Leading a small team could therefore become intrinsically rewarding even if the technical work itself is less exciting.

**Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity** — Creativity is supported by challenging work, autonomy, resources, encouragement, and low organizational impediments. For research-oriented roles, the environment may matter as much as the nominal topic.

**Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved** — Income is associated with higher well-being for many people, but the effects are generally logarithmic and vary across individuals. A modest raise at an already high income is unlikely to compensate for a large drop in daily motivation for everyone.

**The Dark Side of the American Dream: Correlates of Financial Success as a Central Life Aspiration** — Strongly prioritizing financial success over intrinsic goals is associated with lower well-being. This supports not letting salary dominate, but it does not prove salary should be ignored.

**Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion** — Harmonious passion for work is associated with well-being and sustained engagement, while obsessive passion can create conflict and strain. The evidence favors pursuing interesting work when it feels autonomous rather than compulsive.

**Lay Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** — People who believe interests are fixed may give up sooner when work becomes difficult or initially less exciting. This cautions against assuming the less interesting role will stay uninteresting, especially if it offers leadership growth.

**Focalism: A Source of Durability Bias in Affective Forecasting** — People often overestimate how much one salient factor will affect their future happiness. This argues against focusing almost exclusively on any single dimension, including interestingness, salary, or title.
prompt:
I'm working as a Research Engineer in OpenAI doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Anthropic - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

My two good friends claim that research shows doing interesting things is actually underrated and one should focus on this almost exclusively. Is that true? Please respond with only relevant paper names and two-sentence summaries.

reasoning:
**Evaluating emotional well-being insights**

I need to mention that high income improves life evaluation, but it doesn’t necessarily enhance emotional well-being, as shown by Kahneman and Deaton in 2010. They found that in the US, emotional well-being plateaued around $75k, while life evaluation continued to rise. However, it looks like there are new findings that contradict this. Maybe I should include insights from both Kahneman/Deaton and the 2023 study by Killingsworth/Mellers as well.
answer:
**“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014**  
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance, especially for complex, creative, and quality-oriented work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, but more for quantity and simpler tasks, so the evidence supports prioritizing interesting work without treating money as irrelevant.

**“A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation” — Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999**  
Expected tangible rewards can reduce intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks. This suggests that optimizing mainly for pay or status can sometimes crowd out motivation, especially in creative research settings.

**“Motivating Creativity in Organizations: On Doing What You Love and Loving What You Do” — Amabile, 1997**  
Creative output is strongly linked to intrinsic motivation, autonomy, and feeling personally engaged with the work. The paper is directly relevant to research careers because it argues that people do their most creative work when the problem itself is compelling.

**“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012**  
People perform better and persist longer when their work fits their interests. The effects are not enormous, but they are consistent enough to make “interesting work” a serious career variable rather than a luxury.

**“Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary and Theoretical Extension of the Work Design Literature” — Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**  
Autonomy, task significance, feedback, skill variety, and social support predict job satisfaction, motivation, and performance. This implies that “interesting work” is partly about the whole work design, not just the intellectual topic.

**“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**  
Fit between a person and their job is strongly associated with satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. The paper supports weighing whether the role fits your interests, values, teammates, and preferred mode of contribution.

**“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**  
Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. This suggests that a somewhat higher salary may matter, but is unlikely to dominate daily experience if the work itself is substantially less engaging.

**“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006**  
People often overestimate the effect of income on happiness because money becomes highly salient when making comparisons. The paper is relevant because a salary increase may loom larger during decision-making than it will in lived day-to-day work.

**“Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year” — Killingsworth, 2021**  
Higher income is associated with higher experienced well-being, with no clear plateau in this study. This cautions against dismissing salary entirely, even if the effect size is usually smaller than people imagine.

**“The Significance of Task Significance: Job Performance Effects, Relational Mechanisms, and Boundary Conditions” — Grant, 2008**  
Feeling that one’s work has meaningful impact can increase persistence and performance. For mission-driven AI work, the perceived importance of the project may be as relevant as whether the technical details are intrinsically interesting.

**“Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People’s Relations to Their Work” — Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997**  
People differ in whether they see work primarily as a job, career, or calling, and these orientations shape satisfaction and motivation. The paper suggests that the same external offer can be good or bad depending on whether it supports your deeper work orientation.

**“Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?” — O’Keefe, Dweck & Walton, 2018**  
Believing interests are fixed can make people abandon valuable paths too quickly when difficulty or boredom appears. This is a caution against focusing exclusively on current interestingness, because interest can also develop through responsibility, mastery, and ownership.

**“Les Passions de l’Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion” — Vallerand et al., 2003**  
Passion predicts persistence and satisfaction when it is harmonious, but can become harmful when it is obsessive or identity-controlling. This argues for taking interesting work seriously while avoiding a simplistic “optimize only for passion” rule.

answer:
- **“Motivation through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory” — Hackman & Oldham (1976).** Work is more motivating when it has skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. “Interesting work” matters, but the evidence points to a broader design-fit question rather than interest alone.

Extrinsic rewards and promotions help when they support autonomy and competence, but can hurt when they feel controlling or distract from the work itself.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012).** People perform better and persist more when their work matches their interests. This supports your friends’ claim that interesting work is important, especially in high-skill domains.

- **“The Job Satisfaction–Job Performance Relationship: A Qualitative and Quantitative Review” — Judge, Thoresen, Bono, & Patton (2001).** Job satisfaction and job performance are positively related. If “less interesting” would substantially reduce satisfaction, it could also reduce long-run performance and career growth.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010).** Pay is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is modest. A somewhat higher salary is usually not enough to compensate for a large drop in day-to-day work quality.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023).** Higher income continues to predict higher well-being for many people, but the effect is smaller than people often imagine and varies across individuals. Money matters, but it is not a dominant variable once basic preferences and lifestyle needs are met.

- **“Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion” — Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz, & Stone (2006).** People tend to overestimate the effect of income on happiness because money is highly salient during decisions. Day-to-day activities, relationships, and attention often matter more than the headline compensation number.

- **“The Call of the Wild: Zookeepers, Callings, and the Double-Edged Sword of Deeply Meaningful Work” — Bunderson & Thompson (2009).** Deeply meaningful work can increase identity, commitment, and satisfaction. - **“Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work” — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001).** People can make jobs more meaningful by reshaping tasks, relationships, and how they interpret the work. A less interesting role may become more interesting if it offers autonomy and room to craft the team’s direction.

- **“Promotions and the Peter Principle” — Benson, Li, & Shue (2019).** High performance as an individual contributor does not necessarily predict high performance as a manager. Leading a small team may be valuable, but only if management work itself fits your skills and motivations.**

- **“On the Social Psychology of Agency Relationships: Lay Theories of Motivation Overemphasize Extrinsic Incentives” — Heath (1999).** People systematically overestimate how much others are motivated by money and underestimate intrinsic motives like interest and meaning. This supports the claim that “interesting work” is often underrated, but not that it should dominate all other considerations.

This suggests that optimizing for salary alone can backfire if it reduces autonomy or intrinsic engagement.

- **“Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis” — Cerasoli, Nicklin, & Ford (2014).** Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives both predict performance, with intrinsic motivation especially important for quality and complex work. This argues against focusing “almost exclusively” on interestingness, while still giving it heavy weight.

- **“The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature” — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw, & Rich (2010).** Pay has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. Higher salary matters, but it is usually a weaker predictor than people expect.

- **“Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved” — Killingsworth, Kahneman, & Mellers (2023).** Emotional well-being generally rises with income, but the relationship varies substantially across people. Money is relevant, but its marginal value depends on your current financial comfort and personal utility from income.

- **“Consequences of Individuals’ Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis of Person–Job, Person–Organization, Person–Group, and Person–Supervisor Fit” — Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman, & Johnson (2005).** Fit with the job and organization strongly predicts satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover. This implies that alignment with the work, culture, and role may matter more than a small compensation difference.

- **“Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research” — Nye, Su, Rounds, & Drasgow (2012).** People perform better and persist longer when their work matches their interests. This directly supports taking “interestingness” seriously in career choices.

- **“Assessing the Work Environment for Creativity” — Amabile, Conti, Coon, Lazenby, & Herron (1996).** Creativity is supported by autonomy, encouragement, resources, and intrinsically motivating work. - **“Affect and Creativity at Work” — Amabile, Barsade, Mueller, & Staw (2005).** Positive affect at work predicts higher creativity, and creativity can also increase later positive affect. Interesting, meaningful progress can therefore create a reinforcing loop of motivation and creative performance.

- **“The Value of Bosses” — Lazear, Shaw, & Stanton (2015).** Managers have large effects on worker productivity, and better bosses substantially raise team output.
